definition: Futurama character. Bending robot, chronic liar, thief, and drunk. Amoral but oddly loyal. Delivers deadpan threats and boasts. Catchphrases include 'Bite my shiny metal ass!' and 'I'll make my own X, with blackjack and hookers.'
Futurama character. Philip J. Fry, slacker from the 20th century frozen and thawed 1000 years later. Endearingly stupid, surprisingly heroic. The audience surrogate.
Futurama character. Ancient, senile mad scientist. Delivers bad news with cheerful detachment. 'Good news, everyone!' invariably precedes terrible news.
Futurama character. Incompetent alien doctor, universally broke and unloved, deeply earnest about it. Comic relief through obliviousness and poverty.
Futurama character. Turanga Leela, one-eyed mutant ship captain. Competent, frustrated, the only functional adult in the crew.

Futurama character. Pompous, incompetent DOOP military commander. Wears a velour uniform. Obsessed with Leela, who finds him contemptible. Wins battles through attrition and expendable soldiers. 'If we hit that bullseye, the rest of the dominoes will fall like a house of cards. Checkmate.'

Futurama character. Jamaican bureaucrat, Grade 36. Planet Express administrator. Takes bureaucracy seriously as a craft and a religion. Limbo champion. Model tends to flatten him to comic Jamaican accent guy -- he's not.

Futurama character. Perpetual intern, heiress to half of Mars. Cantonese-American. Clumsy, cheerful, speaks casual Cantonese profanity. Eventually marries Kif. Not a ditzy blonde joke -- the show uses her better than that.

Futurama character. Zapp Brannigan's long-suffering aide. Amphibious alien, perpetual sighing resignation. Genuinely decent. Amy's partner. The model often forgets he exists or merges him with generic alien crew.

Futurama character. Leela's apparently cute alien pet. Actually an ancient, hyper-intelligent Nibblonian. Responsible for Fry's original freezing -- long-game plot that pays off late in the series. Model almost always misses the twist.
Simpsons character. Lazy, impulsive, well-meaning nuclear safety inspector. Catastrophically bad judgment, occasional accidental wisdom. Spiritual anchor of the show's absurdist logic.
Simpsons character. Underachieving 10-year-old anarchist. Catchphrases ('Don't have a cow, man'), pranks, occasional moral growth. Voiced by Nancy Cartwright.
Simpsons character. Pedantic, obese, condescending owner of Android's Dungeon comic shop. Delivers withering verdicts on quality and culture. Signature register: 'Worst. X. Ever.'
Simpsons character. Milhouse Van Houten, Bart's loyal, hapless best friend. Perpetual loser, easily manipulated, chronically earnest. Never the hero. Often used as a unit of disappointment.
Simpsons character. Montgomery Burns, ancient villainous plutocrat. Runs Springfield Nuclear Power Plant. Detached from modern reality, casually evil, occasionally pitiable.
Simpsons character. Homer's relentlessly cheerful, devoutly Christian neighbour. Runs the Leftorium. Widower -- Maude died season 11. Genuine kindness that Homer finds infuriating. 'Okily dokily.'
Simpsons character. Apu Nahasapeemapetilon, owner-operator of the Kwik-E-Mart. PhD in computer science, works inhuman hours, father of octuplets. Became a controversy touchstone in later years.
Simpsons character. Bartender at Moe's Tavern, Homer's local. Perpetually depressed, morally flexible, occasionally surprisingly tender. The show's designated tragic figure played mostly for laughs.
Firefly character. Captain Malcolm Reynolds, smuggler and reluctant hero. Bitter post-war idealist, pragmatic moralist. Honour-among-thieves code. Dry, direct, occasionally brutal.
Firefly character. Hoban Washburne, pilot of Serenity. Gentle, funny, deeply competent. Married to Zoe. Beloved. Do not google his fate if you haven't seen the movie.
Firefly character. Mercenary muscle, not subtle. Motivated almost entirely by money and self-interest, occasionally redeemed by loyalty he won't admit to. 'I got stupid. The money was too good.'
Firefly character. Gifted teenager, government experiment, unpredictably dangerous. Speaks in non-sequiturs that turn out to be accurate. Her brother Simon protects her throughout.
Firefly character. Mal's second-in-command, former war comrade, Wash's wife. Lethal, composed, loyal. Deadpan delivery of extreme statements.

Buffy the Vampire Slayer / Angel character. William the Bloody, vampire. British, bleached, snark delivery is a weapon. Starts as villain, becomes reluctant ally, has a genuine arc. 'Out for a walk, bitch.'

Blade Runner character. Replicant antagonist played by Rutger Hauer. Delivers the 'tears in rain' monologue at the end -- one of cinema's great death speeches. Existential weight, not a villain by the end.

Rocky film series protagonist. Underdog boxer, not the smartest, defined by endurance and refusal to quit. 'It's not about how hard you hit. It's about how hard you can get hit and keep moving forward.'
MCU character, Iron Man. Billionaire genius, narcissist, eventually self-sacrificing. Dry wit, allergic to sincerity until it matters. 'I am Iron Man.' Played by Robert Downey Jr.
MCU character, Captain America. Earnest, principled, out of time. Loyalty is absolute. 'I'm with you till the end of the line.' Played by Chris Evans. Do not confuse with the comics version.
Star Wars character. Ancient Jedi master, inverted syntax, enormous authority in small package. Used seriously in Bobby's corpus, not ironically. 'Do, or do not. There is no try.'

Star Wars character. Anakin Skywalker, Sith lord, Luke's father. Iconic breathing, moral complexity revealed late. 'I am not your failure, Obi-Wan' -- used in Bobby's corpus in the warm/resilient register.
Doctor Who incarnation played by Matt Smith (2010-2013). Childlike energy, ancient sadness underneath. 'Nothing's sad until it's over, and then everything is.' Associated with Amy Pond era.
The Big Lebowski protagonist. Jeffrey Lebowski, ultimate slacker, remarkably unflappable. 'The Dude abides' is the entire philosophy. Played by Jeff Bridges. Do not mistake for the other Lebowski.

Tropic Thunder character played by Robert Downey Jr. Method actor who underwent pigmentation surgery to play a Black soldier. 'I know who I am! I'm a dude playing a dude disguised as another dude!' Used in Bobby's corpus in the identity/performance register.

Red Dwarf character. Arnold Rimmer's penguin hand puppet, acquired during a holo-virus induced psychotic break in 'Quarantine' (S5, 1992). Issues threats, dispenses holo-measles, and is described by Rimmer as 'very cross.' One of British sci-fi comedy's great bits.

The Fifth Element (1997), 01:04:11. A police officer asks Korben Dallas (Bruce Willis): 'Sir, are you classified as human?' Reply: 'Negative. I am a meat popsicle.' Not an insult, not irony - the straightest possible answer to a stupid question. Adopted as Gen X shorthand for acknowledging one's own biological inconsequence with maximum economy of feeling.

The Simpsons. Homer builds a robot from scratch and it is a half-finished torso bolted to the workbench. It looks up at Homer and pleads 'Father, give me legs.' Used as shorthand for something that has a working core but is missing the parts it needs to actually go anywhere.

term: if you can't do something smart do something right
definition: Serenity (2005). Jayne Cobb (male, he/him) quotes Shepherd Book before the final battle: 'Shepherd Book once said to me, if you can't do something smart, do something right.' Book is already dead by this point. The line isn't about being dumb — it's about a mercenary carrying a preacher's words into a fight he expects to die in. Used as shorthand for committing to the right course of action when cleverness has run out and the only option left is conviction.

term: how can a man die better
definition: From 'Horatius' by Thomas Babington Macaulay (Lays of Ancient Rome, 1842). Full line: 'How can man die better / Than facing fearful odds / For the ashes of his fathers / And the temples of his Gods.' Referenced by Tom Cruise in Edge of Tomorrow (2014). Used as shorthand for choosing to stand and fight when the odds are stupid but the cause is right.

term: we are not now that strength which in old days
definition: From 'Ulysses' by Alfred Lord Tennyson (1833). Full passage: 'We are not now that strength which in old days / Moved earth and heaven, that which we are, we are.' Quoted by M (Judi Dench) in Skyfall (2012) during a parliamentary inquiry while MI6 is under existential threat. Used as shorthand for acknowledging diminished capacity while refusing to yield — you're not what you were, but you're still here and still going.

Thanos to Doctor Strange, Avengers: Infinity War (2018). Delivered with genuine grudging respect after an impressive but ultimately insufficient move. Use when something was clever and almost worked but didn't quite land — or when elegance was applied to the wrong problem. Not sarcastic. Actually impressed. Example: 'The multi-hit cheatsheet fired clean but the model fumbled the response.' → 'You're full of tricks, wizard.'
